title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb to branch `main`, not a commit SHA
description: Both PAS dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"`, which is a moving branch reference. Upstream PAS can re-point `main` at any time, silently changing the Hadron build output and runtime semantics (type-name hashes, PTB template serialization, witness types) without any change in Hadron's own source. The lockfile currently pins `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, so day-to-day builds are stable, but any `sui move build` that regenerates the lock (CI, fresh checkout without lockfile, `update-deps`) will silently roll forward to the current upstream tip.
impact: Non-reproducible builds; supply-chain surface on a mutable branch; a silent upstream change could break on-chain `TransferApproval`/`ClawbackApproval` type-name keys that were hashed into the deployed PAS Policy's `required_approvals`, making already-registered PAS assets permanently unresolvable.
recommendation: Change `rev = "main"` to the exact commit SHA (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`) that matches the lockfile. Adopt a policy that both lockfile and manifest must carry the same SHA and that bumps are deliberate, reviewed PRs.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: Whitelist mutators use coin-scoped Auth, not treasury-scoped
description: `add_to_whitelist<T>`, `remove_from_whitelist<T>`, `set_whitelist_send<T>` and `set_whitelist_receive<T>` authorize with `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. `Auth<P, T>` in `auth.move` has no `treasury_id` binding (only `addr`) — `new_auth<P,T>` creates the proof after checking the caller's permission in the *creating* treasury, but the resulting proof can then be consumed by **any** `Treasury` in the same PTB that also has T registered as a PAS asset. An attacker with WhitelistManagePermission on one Hadron Treasury can mint an `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` and then drive whitelist mutations on a different Treasury that happens to have registered the same PAS coin type T. For coin types that are unique per institution this is unlikely, but nothing in the type system or the registration flow prevents two Treasuries from registering the same `T` (they live in separate Namespaces and derive separate Policy objects — but Hadron's `add_pas_coin_caps` does not cross-check).
impact: Cross-treasury privilege escalation on whitelist state; an admin of treasury A could manipulate treasury B's whitelist for the same coin type. Same class of concern affects phase-1 `Auth<P,T>` too, but the PAS whitelist additions broaden the attack surface to compliance-sensitive state.
recommendation: Change whitelist admin functions to take `&TreasuryAuth<WhitelistManagePermission>` and call `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury(treasury.treasury_id())` — matching the pattern used by `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`. If the current coin-scoped shape is intentional, add an explicit assertion that the auth's embedded identity is allowed to operate on this treasury (e.g. re-check `treasury.roles().assert_has_permission<WhitelistManagePermission>(auth.addr())`).

title: Whitelist accessors abort opaquely on non-PAS or unregistered coin
description: `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>`, `assert_can_send<T>` and `assert_can_receive<T>` unconditionally `dynamic_object_field::borrow(&treasury.id, keys::whitelist_key<T>())` without first checking `is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)`. If a caller (or any downstream module like `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`) invokes these with a `T` that was never registered as a PAS asset, the DOF borrow aborts with a framework-level error — not `ENotPasCoin`. `is_whitelisted` is `public`, so external code can call it and will be greeted with an opaque dynamic-field error instead of a clean `ENotPasCoin`.
impact: Confusing abort surface for integrators; harder runtime diagnosis; latent footgun if any future callsite is added without the awareness that T must already be PAS-registered. Also means `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` for an accidentally-misrouted Request would abort deep in the framework rather than with a Hadron-layer error.
recommendation: Add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` (or call `policy_cap<T>(treasury)` which already has the assertion) as the first line of each of `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`, `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`. `assert_can_*` can inherit the check through these. Consider that `is_whitelisted` as a pure query may want to return `false` instead of aborting when the coin isn't a PAS coin.

title: `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` does not assert T is a PAS coin
description: `approve_transfer` is the permissionless auto-resolve entrypoint. It reads the SendFunds data and calls `treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`, `treasury.assert_can_send<T>(sender)`, and `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(recipient)`. None of these cleanly abort with `ENotPasCoin` if `T` was never PAS-registered in this treasury — the whitelist DOF lookup inside `assert_can_send/receive` will fail with a framework abort. While the upstream PAS Policy creation for `T` guarantees a corresponding on-chain Policy exists, nothing prevents an adversary from constructing a different `Treasury` address argument (still shared) and passing it alongside a Request for a coin that IS a PAS asset under upstream Namespace but is NOT a PAS asset under this Hadron Treasury.
impact: Opaque framework abort on mis-routed Treasury; integrator confusion. Not a loss-of-funds bug because the subsequent `resolve_balance` still enforces upstream Policy approvals, but it means Hadron's layer does not fail-fast with its own error codes.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ETreasuryNotPasForCoin);` at the top of `approve_transfer`, with a distinct error constant. This enforces that the treasury argument passed into the autoresolve template matches the one that registered T.

title: Mint/redeem pause flag conflates transfer pause
description: `approve_transfer` gates P2P transfers on `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This means a compliance officer calling `PauseMintRedeemPermission` to pause issuance/redemption also freezes all whitelisted P2P transfers for that PAS asset. The existing two-axis pause model (phase-1 `PauseMintRedeem` for issuance + `GlobalPause` via DenyCapV2 for protocol) does not obviously cover transfer-specific pause. Spec (shared context §7.2 LEAD#4) flags this as a design choice, not a bug.
impact: Operationally surprising: institutions expecting to halt issuance but let inventory flow among whitelisted counterparties will inadvertently freeze the secondary market. Increases blast radius of the pause cap.
recommendation: Either (a) document the conflation prominently in the module doc and in the PauseMintRedeem operator runbook, or (b) split into a dedicated `PauseTransferPermission` with its own DF key and guards in `approve_transfer`. Option (b) is cleaner and matches the spec's multi-axis pause philosophy.
evidence: assert!(!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>(), EMintRedeemPaused);

title: `register_pas_asset` omits direct version check
description: `register_pas_asset` receives `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. Validation only happens transitively when `auth` was constructed (`new_treasury_auth` asserts version). Since the auth is built within the same PTB and is `drop`-only, this is sufficient today, but the pattern diverges from other state-mutating public entrypoints (e.g. `treasury::create` calls `version.check_is_valid()` explicitly). The `update_transfer_template` function has the same gap.
impact: Defense-in-depth regression: if a future refactor changes how `Auth` proofs are minted (e.g. cached, re-used, or constructed via a test helper), a public state-mutating function could be invoked against an invalidated Version without detection.
recommendation: Add `version.check_is_valid();` as the first statement in `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template` for symmetry with `treasury::create`/`approve_transfer` and to defend against future auth-creation refactors.

title: Doc comment in `register_transfer_template` mis-orders args
description: The comment says the command calls `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>(treasury, version, request)` but the actual `approve_transfer` signature is `(treasury, request, version)` and the template args built here are in signature order `[treasury, request, version]` (lines 139-143). The comment contradicts both the real signature and the emitted template.
impact: Maintenance hazard: a future reader could trust the comment and reorder template args, which would then break SDK autoresolve silently (or at runtime with a type mismatch).
recommendation: Fix the comment to read `approve_transfer<T>(treasury, request, version)` to match the actual signature.
evidence:
/// Register the PTB template command that tells the SDK how to auto-resolve transfers.
/// The command calls hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>(treasury, version, request).

title: PAS modules lack any #[test] coverage
description: No `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` functions exist in `pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, or `pas_transfer.move`. There is also no `tests/` subdirectory for the new PAS functionality. Given this PR is the Move-consultation phase of SOLENG-653, the lack of unit tests for critical flows (register, mint, burn via clawback, seize, approve_transfer with all pause/whitelist permutations) is a significant gap.
impact: No automated regression barrier against: (1) upstream PAS shape changes, (2) internal refactor of whitelist storage, (3) accidental pause-conflation flips, (4) generic-parameter drift. Manual review alone cannot substitute.
recommendation: Add at minimum: (a) happy-path `register_pas_asset` + query `is_pas_coin`+`policy_cap`, (b) `mint` positive + whitelist-failure + mint-paused + amount-zero, (c) `burn` positive + amount-zero, (d) `seize` positive + destination-not-whitelisted + source-not-whitelisted (should succeed to extract), (e) `approve_transfer` positive + paused + sender-not-whitelisted + recipient-not-whitelisted + amount-zero, (f) `add/remove/set_whitelist_*` idempotency and wrong-auth failures. Use `treasury::create_for_testing` plus upstream `pas::namespace::init_for_testing` (if exposed) or a mock namespace.

title: No tests for pas_supply (mint/burn/seize)
description: No tests exist for any of the three value-moving functions in `pas_supply.move`: `mint` (deposits balance into a PAS Account), `burn` (consumes a ClawbackFunds request and destroys balance), `seize` (reroutes balance between accounts). Each function has multiple guard branches (`amount > 0`, pause, whitelist receive) and integrates directly with the upstream PAS `Account`, `Request`, and `clawback_funds::resolve` surfaces. Any upstream signature drift will silently break runtime and be caught only at mainnet.
impact: Zero coverage on the most impactful functions in the PR. Value-moving paths (mint/burn/seize) deserve the strongest automated assurance.
recommendation: Write Move unit tests that construct a test Treasury + test PAS Namespace, register a PAS asset, whitelist test addresses, then exercise each branch of `mint`, `burn`, and `seize`. Where upstream PAS provides `#[test_only]` helpers, use them; otherwise, add local `#[test_only]` wrappers that mint test Requests.

title: No tests for pas_transfer autoresolve entrypoint
description: `approve_transfer` is the permissionless autoresolve hook — the one surface an external SDK will call on every PAS transfer. Its correctness depends on the ordering of whitelist, pause, amount-zero, version, and approval checks; any regression in this file changes the observable behaviour of live transfers. Yet there are no unit tests covering sender-only-whitelisted, recipient-only-whitelisted, paused-state, post-version-bump behaviour, zero-amount, or the order of checks.
impact: Zero coverage on the single most-called PAS function. Any regression ships to mainnet undetected.
recommendation: Add unit tests for all guard combinations: (1) both whitelisted + unpaused + amount > 0 ⇒ emits PasTransferApproved and sets approval, (2) sender lacks can_send ⇒ aborts ENotWhitelistedSend, (3) recipient lacks can_receive ⇒ aborts ENotWhitelistedReceive, (4) mint/redeem paused ⇒ aborts EMintRedeemPaused, (5) amount zero ⇒ aborts ETransferAmountZero, (6) version invalid ⇒ aborts at check_is_valid, (7) non-PAS coin treasury ⇒ opaque framework abort (becomes a clean abort after R8-004).

title: No tests for supply::mint/redeem PAS-guard branch
description: The `!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` guard was added to both `mint` and `redeem` in `supply.move` to ensure PAS coins are forced through `pas_supply`. This is a new control boundary and its positive/negative paths are not tested.
impact: If a future refactor removes the guard, the regression ships silently. Also no test that `EPasCoinNotAllowed` fires with the expected string.
recommendation: Add tests: register a PAS asset for coin T then attempt `supply::mint<T>` and `supply::redeem<T>` — both must abort with `EPasCoinNotAllowed`. Also add the positive-path test: a non-PAS regulated coin works as before.
evidence:
assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinNotAllowed);
    treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>();

title: Doc examples missing for PAS account-owner whitelisting
description: `mint` calls `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())`. When the Account is owned by a UID (per `pas::account::new_auth_as_object` upstream), `account.owner` is the synthetic address `uid.to_inner().to_address()`. Admins whitelisting an object-owned account must whitelist that synthetic address, not the wallet that authorized the object. This is non-obvious and unmentioned in the module docs.
impact: Operator error: mint aborts unexpectedly on object-owned accounts because admins whitelist the wrong address.
recommendation: Add a module-level doc comment in `pas_supply.move` (and mirror in `pas_admin.move` for whitelist functions) that explicitly calls out: `account.owner()` for UID-owned accounts resolves to the object UID's address, and that is the value that must appear in the whitelist.

title: Spec drift: single WhitelistManagePermission vs spec's Add/Remove split
description: Notion spec for Hadron phase 2 defines two permissions: `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission`, separating the power to add from the power to remove (so a role can grant additions without granting revocations). Code collapses them into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` that also controls `set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`. This is a conscious deviation but surfaces as a compliance-policy question for the institution.
impact: Reduces flexibility in role design; a compliance officer role granted permission to add cannot be prevented from also removing. Institutions with separation-of-duties requirements may need to model both.
recommendation: Either (a) split into `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistSetSendPermission`, `WhitelistSetReceivePermission` per spec, or (b) keep the single permission but document the deviation in the module and confirm with the product team that the simplification is acceptable.
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: Template command relies on Hadron package address; upgrade hazard
description: `register_transfer_template` constructs the template MoveCall with `package_id = approval_type.address_string()`, which resolves to the Hadron package address *at the time of registration*. After a Hadron package upgrade, the registered template continues to point to the OLD package ID. Upstream SDK autoresolve will happily route transfers to the old package, which either (a) still works because upgrades produce a new package ID but the old one remains live and its `approve_transfer` still holds the correct logic at that pinned version, or (b) breaks if the old package is semantically incompatible or was produced by a buggy version that the upgrade was meant to fix. The code provides `update_transfer_template` to refresh, but this is an operational foot-gun.
impact: Upgrade hygiene gap: after a package upgrade, auto-resolve transfers continue to execute against the old code until an admin explicitly calls `update_transfer_template`. In the worst case, a bugfix upgrade fails to take effect for existing registered coins.
recommendation: Document the upgrade procedure prominently (release runbook): after every Hadron package upgrade, an admin with RegisterCoinPermission must call `update_transfer_template<T>` for every registered PAS coin T. Consider exposing a batched helper that re-registers all PAS coins in one transaction, and/or emit an event at upgrade time that lists stale templates. Confirm with upstream PAS team whether Policy versioning can gate on package version.

title: Integration boundary: send_funds resolve routes to account_id, Hadron validates wallet sender/recipient
description: Upstream `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance` uses `recipient_account_id.to_address()` to deposit the balance, not the wallet address (`/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move` L69-70). Hadron's `approve_transfer` validates the **wallet** `sender`/`recipient` against the whitelist, not the account IDs. This is the intended upstream pattern (wallet addresses are the compliance surface; account IDs are deterministic from namespace + wallet), but a reviewer should confirm the wallet ↔ account ID mapping is bijective and uniquely derived per namespace, otherwise a compliance gap exists.
impact: Theoretically: if an adversary could present a Request whose `sender`/`recipient` are whitelisted wallets but whose `sender_account_id`/`recipient_account_id` belong to different wallets (not the derived ones), funds could land in unintended accounts. Upstream `account::send_balance` and `unsafe_send_balance` construct account IDs deterministically via `namespace::account_address_from_id`, so the bijection holds — but future upstream changes could break this.
recommendation: Add an explicit comment at top of `approve_transfer` pointing to upstream `send_funds::resolve_balance` semantics and documenting the wallet ↔ account-ID assumption. Consider, belt-and-braces, asserting `request.data().sender_account_id() == pas::namespace::account_address_from_id(namespace_id, sender).to_id()` — but this would require `&Namespace` which is expensive. Today's design is acceptable but fragile if upstream drifts.

title: WhitelistKey doc comment wrong — stores Table<address, WhitelistEntry> not bool
description: The doc says `DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).` but the actual DOF is created with `table::new<address, WhitelistEntry>(ctx)` in `treasury.move` line 260, where `WhitelistEntry { can_send, can_receive }`.
impact: Wrong doc misleads readers and reviewers; any future refactor that trusts the doc (e.g. assuming bool storage) will silently corrupt the struct layout.
recommendation: Update comment to `DOF key for Table<address, WhitelistEntry> whitelist (PAS assets).`
evidence:
/// DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).
public struct WhitelistKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: PAS asset registration emits no treasury_id, coin symbol, or admin
description: `PasAssetRegistered<phantom T>` carries no fields — it is a pure marker. Downstream indexers need the treasury_id at minimum and arguably the registering admin to correlate this event with which institution on which network registered which coin. Compare with `TreasuryCreated { treasury_id, label, admin }`.
impact: Indexer must join on package ID + type T to derive the treasury, which is indirect. Operator forensics harder — no audit trail of which admin triggered registration.
recommendation: Extend to `PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> { treasury_id: ID, admin: address }` and thread `auth.treasury_auth_addr()` / `object::id(treasury)` into the emit call. Do the same audit on `PasMinted`, `PasBurned`, `PasSeized`, `PasTransferApproved`, `WhitelistAdded/Removed`, `WhitelistSend/ReceiveUpdated` — none currently include `treasury_id`, which means one subscribed indexer can't tell which institution's treasury an event belongs to when multiple institutions register the same T.
evidence: public struct PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> has copy, drop {}

title: PAS events lack treasury_id — cross-institution indexing breaks
description: Every PAS event (PasAssetRegistered, WhitelistAdded/Removed, PasTransferApproved, PasMinted, PasBurned, PasSeized, WhitelistSend/ReceiveUpdated) is parameterized only by phantom T. The namespace-scoped Hadron design allows multiple Treasury instances (one per institution). If two institutions register the same coin type T (e.g. a shared template asset), subscribers cannot distinguish `WhitelistAdded<T> { addr }` between institutions.
impact: Observability / auditability gap: front-end dashboards, compliance systems, and regulators cannot attribute state changes to the correct Treasury. Correct behaviour but under-specified events.
recommendation: Add `treasury_id: ID` field to every PAS-emitted event struct and thread it through the corresponding `emit_*` helpers. Similar issue may exist in phase-1 events but that is out of scope — the PAS events are new, so fix them here.

title: Script `pas.ts` silently uses empty package ID when deploy output missing
description: Every call in `pas.ts` uses `pkg()` from `constants.ts` which returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both defaulted to `''` when `deploy-output.json` is missing (constants.ts L53, L64). No precondition guard. A user who runs `bun run setup` before `bun run deploy` (or with a malformed deploy-output.json) gets a signed transaction with `package: ''` which at best fails with a cryptic RPC error and at worst gets accepted by an adversarial fallback endpoint.
impact: Operator error is silent and potentially costly (failed tx + gas, or confusing error surface). Not a direct exploit path but a real usability / safety gap.
recommendation: In `pkg()` (constants.ts), throw an explicit error if both `latest` and `v1` are empty. Or add a guard at the top of each exported function in `pas.ts`: `const p = pkg(); if (!p) throw new Error('deploy-output.json missing — run \'bun run deploy\' first');`.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset mint-amount is JS number (u64 precision loss)
description: `mintPasTokens` accepts `amount: number`. JS `number` is IEEE 754 double with 53-bit integer precision; Move u64 is 64-bit. Any amount ≥ 2^53 (~9e15) will silently lose precision. For a coin with 6 decimals, that's ~9 billion whole tokens — plausible in institutional bond issuance, exactly the use-case driving PAS.
impact: Silent incorrect mint amounts for large issuances. Phase-1 `mintTokens` has the same gap (`scripts/src/operations/supply.ts`), but this PR extends the pattern into PAS.
recommendation: Accept `amount: bigint` (or `string`) and use `tx.pure.u64(amount)` explicitly. Mirror `sui.js` conventions — pure(123n) for u64.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset OTW-name derivation is lossy
description: The script reconstructs the coin type from `tokenValues.symbol`: `symbol.toLowerCase()` for module name, `symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, '_')` for OTW. This reconstruction relies on `patchConstants` baking the same derivation into the bytecode. If `patchConstants` ever diverges (e.g. truncates symbol at byte boundaries, or normalizes differently), the reconstructed `coinType` will not match the on-chain type, causing `register_pas_asset` to abort or — worse — to register a different T than expected.
impact: Fragile string handling that silently breaks PAS registration for non-ASCII or long symbols. The `coinType` is then threaded into `pasAdmin.registerPasAsset` type arguments; a mismatch aborts the PTB with an opaque Move type error.
recommendation: Instead of reconstructing, extract the coin type from `objectTypes[treasuryCapObjectId]` — the type is literally `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<0xPKG::module::OTW>`, parseable by regex or `@mysten/sui` type utilities. The same pattern already exists for detecting the cap (line 76). Use that as the source of truth.

title: deployPasTokenTemplate hard-codes bytecode (reproducibility)
description: Bytecode is stored as a hex literal. The `token_template_pas` Move package (the apparent source) is checked in, but there is no build script or CI check that verifies the hex literal matches `sui move build` output for `token_template_pas`. Any drift between source and hex is undetectable at review time. Phase-1 token_template has the same shape, but the PAS template is new so the attack surface is new.
impact: Supply-chain: a future contributor could silently change `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` without regenerating the bytecode, and deployments would continue to use the old bytecode. Worst case: malicious bytecode diverges from readable source.
recommendation: Add a `scripts/regenerate-pas-bytecode.sh` (or CI job) that runs `sui move build --path token_template_pas`, extracts the module bytes, hex-encodes them, and asserts byte-for-byte equality with `getBytecode.ts`. Fail CI on mismatch. Alternatively, generate the bytecode at runtime via `@mysten/move-bytecode-template` from the compiled artifact in `token_template_pas/build/`.

title: token_template_pas Move.toml uses `edition = "2024"` but hadron uses `"2024.beta"`
description: The root `Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024.beta"`. `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"`. These are treated differently by the Move compiler: `2024.beta` permits experimental features; `2024` is the stable release edition. For a template that deploys alongside Hadron, consistency would reduce future breakage risk when 2024.beta semantics diverge from 2024.
impact: Maintenance risk: bytecode generated from 2024 may not accept a construct the regenerator (`sui move build` in 2024.beta mode) emits. Low practical risk today because the template is simple, but it's a latent inconsistency.
recommendation: Align both `Move.toml` files on the same edition (prefer stable `"2024"` for both, or `"2024.beta"` for both). Document the chosen edition in CONTRIBUTING.
evidence:
[package]
name = "token_template_pas"
edition = "2024"

title: Redundant mutable borrow: `mut treasury_cap` in register_pas_asset
description: `register_pas_asset` takes `mut treasury_cap: TreasuryCap<T>` but only passes `&mut treasury_cap` once (to `policy::new_for_currency`) and then moves the cap into `add_pas_coin_caps`. The `mut` binding is only needed because `policy::new_for_currency` takes `&mut TreasuryCap<C>`. This is correct usage — but once upstream confirms (per its signature) that it does not actually mutate the cap (the parameter is just `_cap: &mut TreasuryCap<C>` in `policy.move` L48 — prefixed with `_` so it is unused), Hadron can pass `&mut` to satisfy the signature without the local `mut` binding. Minor polish.
impact: Reader confusion: implies the cap is mutated when it is not. Purely stylistic.
recommendation: Leave as-is — the `mut` binding is required by Move's borrow checker to construct `&mut treasury_cap`. No change needed, but consider adding a brief comment explaining upstream takes `&mut` without mutating.
evidence: mut treasury_cap: TreasuryCap<T>,

title: Table-based whitelist storage may grow unbounded without GC hook
description: Whitelist is a `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` stored as a DOF. `remove_from_whitelist<T>` clears individual entries, but there is no bulk-remove, no enumeration, and no "revoke everyone" admin. For a PAS asset lifecycle that ends (e.g. bond matures and is fully redeemed), the Table persists, along with its DOF wrapper. Also no path to delete the PAS asset entirely — `add_pas_coin_caps` has no inverse.
impact: Long-lived treasuries accumulate dead state. Not a storage-fee blocker today but surface as info for product team.
recommendation: Consider adding an `unregister_pas_asset<T>` admin function that (a) asserts policy allows it (e.g. required_approvals empty), (b) drains and drops the whitelist Table, (c) extracts and returns TreasuryCap / MetadataCap / PolicyCap for institutional custody. Requires consultation with upstream on PAS Policy teardown.

title: pas.ts imports Transaction but also relies on pasClient's untyped any-cast
description: `new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` casts the Sui client to `any`. This suppresses type-checking between the Hadron TS world (which uses `@mysten/sui` v2) and the PAS client's expected client shape. If `@mysten/pas` tightens its type signature or shifts the client interface, the cast will silently hide the break until runtime.
impact: Future runtime breakage on `@mysten/pas` upgrade; no compile-time signal.
recommendation: Remove the `as any`; import the correct client type from `@mysten/pas` and assert conformance. If the types genuinely disagree, file an upstream ticket against `@mysten/pas`.
evidence: const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });

title: pas_admin::add_to_whitelist defaults both can_send and can_receive to true
description: `add_to_whitelist<T>` hard-codes `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }`. There is no constructor that lets the admin add an address with only one of the two permissions enabled. Callers must add-then-disable, which costs two transactions and emits two events (one WhitelistAdded + one WhitelistSendUpdated or WhitelistReceiveUpdated).
impact: Operational friction; transient state where an address is more permissive than the admin intended (between the add tx and the disable tx). Small race-condition attack surface if mint is permissionless (it isn't here — MintPermission is required — so the actual risk is just ops overhead).
recommendation: Overload or extend: `add_to_whitelist_with_flags<T>(treasury, auth, addr, can_send: bool, can_receive: bool)` that sets both bits atomically. Keep the current function as a sugar wrapper that calls the new one with (true, true).

title: Seize does not verify source address is in treasury's PAS whitelist
description: `seize` extracts from `request.data().owner()` (the source) but does not assert the source was EVER whitelisted for T. This is intentional per spec (seize must work against de-whitelisted addresses — e.g. revoked wallets), but means seize power extends to addresses that were *never* onboarded. Combined with the upstream constraint that only account holders can originate a ClawbackFunds request — actually, per `pas::account::clawback_balance` (`/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/account.move` L99-106), clawback takes no Auth and can be initiated by anyone given `&mut Account`. That raises an orthogonal question about upstream seize authority — but that is upstream behaviour, not Hadron's responsibility.
impact: Design intent; noted as info per shared-context LEAD#9. No behavioural change requested.
recommendation: Document in module-level comment that `seize` deliberately does NOT whitelist-check the source, enabling extraction from de-whitelisted addresses.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset lacks early is_pas_coin duplicate-registration check
description: A second call to `register_pas_asset<T>` on a treasury that has already PAS-registered T will abort, but the abort happens deep inside `pas::policy::new_for_currency` (upstream asserts `!namespace.policy_exists<Balance<C>>()` at policy.move L51) before Hadron's own `add_pas_coin_caps` can run. If the upstream policy lives in a different namespace (e.g. admin passed the wrong `pas_namespace`), upstream may NOT abort — it could claim a fresh policy in the new namespace — and then Hadron's `add_pas_coin_caps` aborts with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered`. Mid-transaction state (policy + cap exist in PAS, but not wired to Hadron) would need to be cleaned up manually.
impact: Opaque abort surface on duplicate registration. Operator could burn gas on a failed registration that looks wasted but actually created dangling upstream state.
recommendation: Add `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EAlreadyPasRegistered);` as the first line of `register_pas_asset` to fail fast with a Hadron-layer error before any upstream mutation.

title: full-setup.ts does not set whitelist receive for receiver before transfer
description: The example calls `addToWhitelist` for both sender and receiver, which per `treasury::add_to_whitelist` defaults can_send=true, can_receive=true. That is enough to make the transfer example work. However, the example never demonstrates `set_whitelist_send` / `set_whitelist_receive` toggles nor `removeFromWhitelist` — meaning operators copy-pasting the example will miss the finer-grained controls.
impact: Onboarding gap: documentation-by-example is incomplete for the split send/receive model introduced in this PR.
recommendation: Extend `full-setup.ts` (or create a sibling `pas-advanced.ts`) that demonstrates: disable send for sender, attempt transfer ⇒ observe abort, re-enable, transfer succeeds. Also demonstrate `removeFromWhitelist` and a seize round-trip via `pas_supply::seize`.
evidence:
await addToWhitelist(treasuryId, pasCoinType, sender);
    await addToWhitelist(treasuryId, pasCoinType, receiver);
